draft.introduction: Cyprian of Carthage’s De Unitate Ecclesiae (251 CE) was written during the crisis following the Decian persecution, when the church was divided over how to treat those who had lapsed. Cyprian’s famous dictum—“you cannot have God as your father unless you have the church as your mother”—shaped all subsequent ecclesiology.

translation.text: The spouse of Christ cannot be defiled; she is uncorrupted and chaste. She knows one home, and guards with virtuous modesty the sanctity of one bedchamber. She keeps us for God; she appoints to the kingdom the sons she has borne. Whoever separates from the church and joins an adulteress is cut off from the promises of the church. He who leaves the church of Christ cannot attain to the rewards of Christ. He is a stranger, a profane person, an enemy. You cannot have God as your father unless you have the church as your mother.
translation.original_text: [Latin: Habere iam non potest Deum patrem qui ecclesiam non habet matrem]

translator_notes.note: Cyprian’s ecclesiology is exclusive and institutional: salvation requires membership in the visible church. This position was invoked by both sides during the Reformation—Catholics to defend institutional unity, Protestants to argue that the “true church” was defined by faithfulness to the gospel rather than by institutional continuity.
